did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning architectures (VGG-19 and ResNet-50) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for melanoma classification. The system utilized CLAHE image enhancement and was deployed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano for real-time inference to classify skin images into melanoma or normal categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a skin disease classification system using VGG-19 and ResNet-50 architectures with CLAHE enhancement. It includes specific sections for Method (dataset collection, preprocessing, model training), Results (performance metrics like accuracy and F1-score), and Conclusion, and was published in the 2025 IEEE International Conference on Networking, Intelligent Systems, and IoT (ICONS-IoT).

title: Edge AI-Based Melanoma Classifier Through CLAHE Enhancement and Deep Learning
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Networking, Intelligent Systems, and IoT (ICONS-IoT)
brief_summary: This study developed an Edge AI-based system for melanoma classification using VGG-19 and ResNet-50 deep learning architectures enhanced by Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE). The models were trained on the ISIC Dataset for melanoma images and the USK-Normal Skin Dataset for normal skin images. Evaluation results demonstrated that CLAHE preprocessing significantly improved performance, with both architectures achieving 100% accuracy, recall, and F1-score. The best-performing models were deployed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano for real-time inference and integrated into a Streamlit-based web interface for user interaction. The research confirms that integrating image enhancement, deep learning, and edge deployment provides an accurate and efficient solution for early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. Sourced from the ISIC Dataset. The study focuses on early detection of this deadly form of skin cancer.
The negative class in the binary classification task. Sourced from the USK-Normal Skin Dataset. Used to distinguish unaffected skin areas from melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Melanoma vs. Normal Skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin images into 'melanoma' or 'normal' categories using VGG-19 and ResNet-50 deep learning architectures. The system outputs predicted disease classes and confidence scores (probabilities) for these discrete classes. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC Archive) containing melanoma skin images.

use.training: Used as training data for melanoma class (1,000 images).
use.validation: Used as validation data for melanoma class (355 images).
use.testing: Used as testing data for melanoma class (355 images).

datasets_used.name: USK-Normal Skin Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from Universitas Syiah Kuala containing normal skin images collected in previous studies.

use.training: Used as training data for normal skin class (1,000 images).
use.validation: Used as validation data for normal skin class (355 images).
use.testing: Used as testing data for normal skin class (355 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described qualitatively (e.g., 'significantly improved') based on observed metric values (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1-score) without statistical inference.

ISIC Dataset (Melanoma) and USK-Normal S.experiment: Binary Classification with VGG-19 and ResNet-50, with and without CLAHE preprocessing
ISIC Dataset (Melanoma) and USK-Normal S.notes: The paper reports that applying CLAHE significantly improved model performance. VGG-19 achieved perfect scores (100%) across all metrics with CLAHE. ResNet-50 also showed improvement, with precision and specificity increasing from 98% to 99% and F1-score from 99% to 100% with CLAHE. The study notes that VGG-19 exhibited slightly better overall performance than ResNet-50 when CLAHE was applied. The best-performing models were deployed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano for real-time inference, demonstrating consistent prediction performance with minimal latency.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (models were trained from scratch or pretraining details were not specified in the provided context)

components: Softmax activation function in the output layer

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layers followed by Softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.output: Probabilities for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Normal Skin)

preprocessing_techniques: CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) applied to all skin images to enhance local contrast, improve visual clarity, highlight important features, and suppress noise in homogeneous areas.
Resizing images to a standardized resolution of 224 × 224 pixels to match input requirements for VGG-19 and ResNet-50 models.
conclusions: Integrating Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) as a preprocessing method significantly improves the classification performance of deep learning models for melanoma detection.
Both VGG-19 and ResNet-50 architectures achieved 100% accuracy, recall, and F1-score when CLAHE enhancement was applied.
Precision and specificity improved with CLAHE, reaching 100% for VGG-19 and 99% for ResNet-50.
VGG-19 exhibited slightly better overall performance than ResNet-50 when CLAHE was applied, achieving consistently higher scores across all metrics.
CLAHE enhances image feature clarity and boosts the model's generalization capability in classifying skin lesion images.
Deploying the best-performing models on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano enables real-time inference with minimal latency and efficient resource usage.
The integration of image enhancement, deep learning, and Edge AI provides an accurate, fast, and reliable solution for early melanoma detection.

The system, integrated with a Streamlit-based web interface, allows users to upload skin images and receive instant classification results with confidence scores, facilitating potential use in low-resource environments.
important_remarks: The study reports perfect performance metrics (100% accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score) for both VGG-19 and ResNet-50 with CLAHE, which is unusually high for medical image classification and may indicate overfitting or a lack of sufficient data diversity.

The dataset is small and balanced (1,000 training images per class), sourced from the ISIC Dataset for melanoma and a specific institutional dataset (USK-Normal Skin Dataset) for normal skin, which limits generalizability to broader populations.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated only on the held-out test split of the provided datasets, raising concerns about real-world robustness.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to support the claim that CLAHE 'significantly' improved performance.
The paper does not specify whether the models were pre-trained (e.g., on ImageNet) or trained from scratch, which is critical for interpreting the high performance on a small dataset.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; it is unclear if the USK-Normal Skin Dataset images overlap with the ISIC dataset or if there was any contamination between training and testing sets beyond the stated split.

The study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. normal skin) and does not address other common skin lesions or differential diagnoses, limiting clinical utility for broader dermatological screening.

Deployment on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano demonstrates feasibility for edge inference, but the system relies on a Streamlit web interface, which may not be suitable for all low-resource clinical environments without internet connectivity.
